questions.question: Why is a pure fanout-on-write strategy usually dangerous for social feeds at large scale?
options: Because ordinary users almost never read their home timeline.
Because celebrity accounts can create extreme write amplification to follower inboxes.
Because push fanout prevents any ranking from happening.
Because home timelines must always be generated at the edge.
questions.explanation: Follower-graph skew is the classic reason pure push breaks down.

questions.question: What is the main product reason to avoid offset pagination in feeds?
options: Feeds change underneath the reader, so inserts and re-ranking make offsets unstable.
Offsets can only be computed inside SQL databases.
Offset pagination always doubles storage cost.
Offsets block moderation deletes from succeeding.
questions.explanation: Cursors are a better match for timelines whose contents and rank evolve continuously.

questions.question: Which architecture choice best handles both ordinary users and celebrity accounts?
options: Use only pull-on-read for everyone.
Use only push fanout for everyone.
Use mixed fanout, pushing ordinary content while reading celebrity content on demand.
Disable ranking so all timelines become cheaper.
questions.explanation: Mixed fanout matches skewed graphs without forcing one global compromise.

questions.question: What is the cleanest fallback when ranking features become stale but the timeline store is healthy?
options: Serve a chronological or rank-lite feed instead of failing the read path.
Reject all timeline reads until the model catches up.
Drop celebrity posts entirely.
Force every user to refollow their graph.
questions.explanation: Graceful degradation matters more than perfect ranking during incidents.

questions.question: Which metric most directly measures feed freshness for recently published content?
options: Time from post creation to first visibility in follower timelines.
Average profile photo size for authors in the graph.
Total number of follows ever created.
Median HTTP request header count.
questions.explanation: Freshness should be measured against the user-visible outcome.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with materialized timeline stores?
options: They improve read latency but create write amplification and invalidation work.
They remove the need for follower-graph storage.
They guarantee perfect ranking relevance.
They make moderation deletes impossible.
questions.explanation: Precomputation buys read speed by paying more work up front.

questions.question: Why are moderation tombstones useful in feed systems?
options: They help suppress deleted or hidden posts even if some materialized entries still exist.
They reduce follower count for celebrity accounts.
They eliminate queueing for fanout workers.
They make cursor pagination unnecessary.
questions.explanation: A tombstone is a fast negative signal while cleanup catches up.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of pull-on-read for high-follower accounts?
options: Reads become more expensive because the feed service must merge and rank more sources at query time.
It forces all posts to be stored only in memory.
It makes freshness impossible to observe.
It guarantees lower p99 latency than precomputed feeds.
questions.explanation: Pull avoids write explosion but shifts work onto feed reads.
